title: [CI:DOCS] s3fs volume mount docs
body:
<!--
Thanks for sending a pull request!

Please make sure you've read our contributing guidelines and how to submit a pull request (https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/CONTRIBUTING.md#submitting-pull-requests).

In case you're only changing docs, make sure to prefix the pull-request title with "[CI:DOCS]". That will prevent functional tests from running and save time and energy.

Finally, be sure to sign commits with your real name. Since by opening
a PR you already have commits, you can add signatures if needed with
something like `git commit -s --amend`.
-->

#### Does this PR introduce a user-facing change?
> Yes
<!--
If no, just write `None` in the release-note block below. If yes, a release note
is required: Enter your extended release note in the block below. If the PR
requires additional action from users switching to the new release, include the
string "action required".

For more information on release notes, please follow the Kubernetes model:
https://git.k8s.io/community/contributors/guide/release-notes.md
-->

Make this github [discussion](https://github.com/containers/podman/discussions/21538) part of the docs

```release-note
adds s3fs mounting example to `podman create volume` docs and to the `podman.unit` docs
```

comments.body:
Need to fix whitespace.
Thanks @WesselAtWork

LGTM
@mheon @Luap99 @TomSweeneyRedHat @edsantiago PTAL
I'm a little iffy on providing full documentation for a plugin we don't ship. Maybe a section describing existing plugins with a link to s3fs instead of full docs?

In scenarios when only one container user ($uid:$gid) needs to write to the volume, I would guess that `--user $uid:$gid --userns uid=$uid,gid=$gid` would work without having to use `allow_other`.   I haven't verified it for the general case for arbitrary UID and GID numbers but at least for the special case `--user $(id -u):$(id -g)  --userns keep-id:uid=$(id -u),gid=$(id -g)` it worked for me when I [tested](https://github.com/containers/podman/discussions/21538#discussioncomment-8461648) rootless Podman from the _main_ git branch and a local minio S3.  I guess if it would work for the general case, then such instructions could also be added in another PR after this PR has landed.


Maybe there should be link to the security warning regarding `allow_other`?
https://github.com/libfuse/libfuse/blob/67d28fb4b762989d895732f8b1aec9a4f69c0d56/README.md?plain=1#L114-L134

@WesselAtWork, thanks for the great Doc update.  As @edsantiago noted, pronouns inside of a man page are strongly frowned upon, especially the word you.  I tried to make them all do a disappearing act, but please do a quick search after you are done to make sure none remain.

Once touched up, this will be a nice addition, so THANKS!

> In scenarios when only one container user ($uid:$gid) needs to write to the volume, I would guess that `--user $uid:$gid --userns uid=$uid,gid=$gid` would work without having to use `allow_other`. I haven't verified it for the general case for arbitrary UID and GID numbers but at least for the special case `--user $(id -u):$(id -g) --userns keep-id:uid=$(id -u),gid=$(id -g)` it worked for me when I [tested](https://github.com/containers/podman/discussions/21538#discussioncomment-8461648) rootless Podman from the _main_ git branch and a local minio S3. I guess if it would work for the general case, then such instructions could also be added in another PR after this PR has landed.
> 
> Maybe there should be link to the security warning regarding `allow_other`? https://github.com/libfuse/libfuse/blob/67d28fb4b762989d895732f8b1aec9a4f69c0d56/README.md?plain=1#L114-L134

I agree.
Using `allow_other` was quick and easy, and in my head, the mount would be located in the `volumes` folder deep inside the podman fs hierarchy meaning it would have "enough" protection. 
If a normal user can't get to any other normal volumes, this one is as "safe" as any other volume.

Including a link to the dangers is good, but I'd like to setup the volume better, I'll do some more experimenting.

UserNS is pretty good. but you are elevating the security of the container user to the mounting user which is fine enough but,
Would like if this was possible to allow the subuid:subgid to access the volume without issues from fuse.

> > In scenarios when only one container user ($uid:$gid) needs to write to the volume, I would guess that `--user $uid:$gid --userns uid=$uid,gid=$gid` would work without having to use `allow_other`. I haven't verified it for the general case for arbitrary UID and GID numbers but at least for the special case `--user $(id -u):$(id -g) --userns keep-id:uid=$(id -u),gid=$(id -g)` it worked for me when I [tested](https://github.com/containers/podman/discussions/21538#discussioncomment-8461648) rootless Podman from the _main_ git branch and a local minio S3. I guess if it would work for the general case, then such instructions could also be added in another PR after this PR has landed.
> > Maybe there should be link to the security warning regarding `allow_other`? https://github.com/libfuse/libfuse/blob/67d28fb4b762989d895732f8b1aec9a4f69c0d56/README.md?plain=1#L114-L134
> 
> I agree. Using `allow_other` was quick and easy, and in my head, the mount would be located in the `volumes` folder deep inside the podman fs hierarchy meaning it would have "enough" protection. If a normal user can't get to any other normal volumes, this one is as "safe" as any other volume.
> 
> Including a link to the dangers is good, but I'd like to setup the volume better, I'll do some more experimenting.
> 
> UserNS is pretty good. but you are elevating the security of the container user to the mounting user which is fine enough but, Would like if this was possible to allow the subuid:subgid to access the volume without issues from fuse.

I had a second thought about `allow_other`. Maybe the security problem discussed in the linked text is only relevant when not running in a user namespace. So no need to add a link. PR looks good as it is. 

A side-note: l also tried `--user $uid:$gid --userns keep-id:uid=$uid,gid=$gid` in the general case with arbitrary `$uid` and `$gid`.  It worked without `allow_other`. To make it work I had to mount the S3 bucket beforehand with `/usr/bin/s3fs` in a subdirectory of the directory that later `podman run` would bind-mount.

First things first: @WesselAtWork you need to clean up trailing whitespace. [Here's why](https://www.mail-archive.com/desktop-devel-list@gnome.org/msg16160.html). There are extensions available for [emacs](https://www.emacswiki.org/emacs/DeletingWhitespace) and [vi](https://vim.fandom.com/wiki/Highlight_unwanted_spaces). You can run `git diff --check` before committing (and/or enable `.git/hooks/pre-commit`) to have git enforce this.

Also, please squash your commits. Thank you!

Hey @edsantiago 
What is the preferred way to represent [Markdown Linebreaks](https://www.markdownguide.org/basic-syntax/#line-break-best-practices)?
If it is critical for two lines to be rendered separately, you could use  separate paragraphs. I encourage you to consider whether how critical that need is.
Cockpit tests failed for commit 458da756b94b322bbc673c99429a2f589a151174. @martinpitt, @jelly, @mvollmer please check.
Anything I still need to check?
@WesselAtWork yes, we've been waiting for you to fix the trailing whitespace errors... did you look at the CI results? Click the "Details" link next to the failed Validate jobs.

@edsantiago So sorry. i literally missed one of them!
Just checking the FreeBSD issue?

Hm. I'll re-run but that seems like it could be infra.
@cevich PTAL

@edsantiago For my two-cents I am not bothered one way or the other.
It's your docs, it would make sense to keep it lean, and focused on podman.

_That being said:_
**podman is a fantastic tool;**  
Having solid examples of how the tool can be used is very valuable.
> When I started fiddling with podman, I didn't quite understand the true breadth of what it is capable of, the more I push it, the more I am surprised.

Having at least one fuse example, the hope is it would result in a "Oh! you can do that?! This will be perfect for mounting this-other-thing!" in the reader

Your concern on the bloating effect for the docs is valid.
Would there be a better home for this?
You mentioned Blogs and a Wiki?
Regardless of whether we accept this I think we'd welcome a blog post about this on [podman.io](https://podman.io/blogs)
This == this PR
It helps if I link the correct blog: https://blog.podman.io/

I am fine with this PR
LGTM
Cockpit tests failed for commit 56c611142410dffb838e9ab6813d03a0ee7ee24c. @martinpitt, @jelly, @mvollmer please check.
Cockpit tests failed for commit ca61e5b329050ad86b238a3a4360ab4d79588704. @martinpitt, @jelly, @mvollmer please check.

Did I do something wrong?
What is up with the broken build?

[APPROVALNOTIFIER] This PR is **APPROVED** This pull-request has been approved by: *edsantiago*, *WesselAtWork* The full list of commands accepted by this bot can be found [here](https://go.k8s.io/bot-commands?repo=containers%2Fpodman). The pull request process is described [here](https://git.k8s.io/community/contributors/guide/owners.md#the-code-review-process)
Needs approval from an approver in each of these files: - ~~[OWNERS](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/OWNERS)~~ [edsantiago] Approvers can indicate their approval by writing `/approve` in a comment Approvers can cancel approval by writing `/approve cancel` in a comment
The blue-robot failure is ok to ignore. There's one typo ("spesify" should be "specify") but otherwise LGTM.

/lgtm

Thank you for your contribution and for your patience!
